options.C: increases.

rationales.A: Diminishing.
rationales.B:
Full statement: sensitivity of a variance swap to changes in implied volatility diminishes over time; as swap approaches expiration, changes in implied volatility have less effect on swap value because remaining time period T - t decreases.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Importantly, the sensitivity of a variance swap to changes in implied volatility diminishes over time.'
rationales.C: Diminishes.

questions.stem: *Total return swap to protect concentrated stock position* *most likely*:

options.A: For the Tundra concentrated position, exchange-traded options provide the complete hedging solution.
options.B: The client should simply sell the 100,000 Tundra shares, since transaction costs are the only consideration.
options.C: example: client holding 100,000 shares of Tundra at €14/share (position €1.4M) with low tax basis, exchange-traded options unavailable.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Listed options are UNAVAILABLE (or insufficient) on the name — that gap is why the OTC total return swap enters.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The LOW TAX BASIS makes an outright sale costly; the TRS sheds the €1.4M exposure without triggering the gain.
rationales.C:
Full statement: example: client holding 100,000 shares of Tundra at €14/share (position €1.4M) with low tax basis, exchange-traded options unavailable; solution: enter equity swap trading Tundra stock return for floating reference rate; agree to exchange total return on shares (price performance and dividends) for reference rate return on same sum of money; must determine time protection is needed and match swap tenor to that period; six-month tenor with reference rate 0.34% annually.

Correct. Per the reading example: 'Bernhard Steinbacher has a client with a holding of 100,000 shares in Tundra Corporation, currently trading for €14 per share. The client has owned the shares for many years and thus has a very low tax basis on this stock. Steinbacher wants to safeguard the value of the position since the client does not want to sell the shares. He cannot find exchange-traded options on the stock... A possible solution is to enter into an equity swap trading the Tundra stock return for the floating reference interest rate. Given Tundra's current share price of €14, the position is worth €1.4 million. Steinbacher can agree to exchange the total return on the shares (which includes the price performance and the dividends received) for the reference rate return on this sum of money. Now he needs to determine the time over which the protection is needed and must match the swap tenor to this period.'

questions.stem: *Tundra Scenario A calc* — €0.10 dividend, price +1%, six months, reference 0.34% *most likely*:

options.B: no calc.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A:
Full statement: total return on stock = [(14 × 1.01) - 14 + 0.10]/14 = 1.71%; six-month reference rate return = 0.17% (half of 0.34%); Tundra total return exceeds reference rate: (1.71% - 0.17%) × €1.4M = €21,620 positive, so client pays swap counterparty €21,620.

Correct. Per Scenario A: 'Over the six months, Tundra pays a €0.10 dividend and the share price rises 1%. The total return on the stock is [(14 × 1.01) − 14 + 0.10]/14 = 1.71%. For a six-month period, the reference rate return would be half the annual rate, or 0.17%. Tundra's total return exceeds the six-month reference rate return: (1.71% − 0.17%) × €1.4 million = €21,620, which is a positive amount, so Steinbacher's client would need to pay the swap counterparty.'
rationales.B: Client pays €21,620.

questions.stem: *Tundra Scenario B* — €0.10 dividend, price -1% *most likely*:

options.A: no calc.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A: Client receives €6,380.
rationales.B:
Full statement: total return on stock = [(14 × 0.99) - 14 + 0.10]/14 = -0.29%; Tundra total return less than six-month reference rate return: (-0.29% - 0.17%) × €1.4M = -€6,380 negative; client receives negative return + reference rate return from counterparty = positive cash inflow of €6,380.

Correct. Per Scenario B: 'Over the six months, Tundra pays a €0.10 dividend and the share price falls 1%. The total return on the stock is [(14 × 0.99) − 14 + 0.10]/14 = −0.29%. Tundra's total return is less than the six-month reference rate return: (−0.29% − 0.17%) × €1.4 million = −€6,380, which is a negative amount, so Steinbacher's client would receive the negative return and the reference rate return from the swap counterparty (meaning the client will receive a positive cash inflow of €6,380).'
rationales.C: €6,380 receive.

questions.stem: *McMillian FTSE 100 cash equitization* £20M cash, FTSE futures £79,000/contract *most likely*:

options.A: no calc.
options.B: no data.

rationales.A: 253 contracts.
rationales.B: 253 futures buy.
rationales.C:
Full statement: N_f = (β_T/β_f)(S/F) = (1.0/1.0)(20,000,000/79,000) = 253.16 ≈ 253 contracts; McMillian buys 253 FTSE 100 futures to gain exposure on notional amount of £20M; both target and futures beta are 1.0; strategy attempts to minimize tracking error by replicating FTSE 100 performance on cash reserves.

Correct. Per the reading: 'McMillian engages in a synthetic index strategy to gain exposure on a notional amount of £20 million to the FTSE 100 by purchasing equity index futures. The number of futures she must purchase is given by the following: Nf = (βT/βf)(S/F) = (1.0/1.0)(20,000,000/79,000) = 253.16 ≈ 253 where the beta of the futures contract, βf, and the target beta, βT, are both equal to 1.0.'

questions.stem: *McMillian outcome after 5% FTSE gain* *most likely*:

options.A: FTSE 100 increases 5%: original £230M UK stocks increases to £241.5M.
options.B: no calc.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A:
Full statement: FTSE 100 increases 5%: original £230M UK stocks increases to £241.5M; FTSE futures rise from £7,900 to £8,282.5; interest on cash at 0.63% floating rate = £20M × 0.63% × 90/360 = £31,500; futures profit = 382.5 index points × £10 × 253 contracts = £967,725; total portfolio value = £241.5M + £20M + £967,725 + £31,500 = £262,499,225; rate of return = £262,499,225/£250M - 1 = 5%; without strategy return would have been just over 4.6%.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Three months later, the FTSE 100 has increased by 5%, and the original value of £230 million invested in UK stocks has increased to £241.5 million. The price of the FTSE 100 Index futures contract has increased to £8,282.5. Interest on the cash invested at the three-month floating rate amounts to £31,500 (£20,000,000 × 0.63% × 90/360). McMillian bought the futures at £7,900, and the cash settlement of the contract at is £8,282.5. So, there is a "gain" of 382.5 index points, each point being worth £10, on 253 contracts for a total cash inflow of £967,725 (382.5 points per contract × £10 per point × 253 contracts). Adding to the portfolio the profit from the futures and the cash reserves plus the interest earned on the cash gives a total market value for McMillian's portfolio of £262,499,225 (= £241,500,000 + £20,000,000 + £967,725 + £31,500). The rate of return for the combined position is: £262,499,225/£250,000,000 − 1 = 0.05, or 5%. Importantly, without implementing this strategy, McMillian's return would have been slightly over 4.6%.'
rationales.C: 5% total.

questions.stem: *Rossi rebalancing €500M portfolio* — reduce stocks 70%→60%, rebalance between Italian/German shares, increase Italian bonds 30%→40% *most likely*:

options.A: The Rossi portfolio holds 60% German equities and 40% French government bonds before rebalancing.
options.B: €500M portfolio 70% stocks (all Italian, β=1.1 vs FTSE MIB) / 30% Italian govt bonds (BTPs, MDUR 6.45).
options.C: Rossi's rebalancing program requires liquidating the underlying Italian shares and BTPs in the cash market.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The starting book is 70% ITALIAN stocks (β 1.1 vs FTSE MIB) and 30% Italian government bonds (BTPs, MDUR 6.45).
rationales.B:
Full statement: €500M portfolio 70% stocks (all Italian, β=1.1 vs FTSE MIB) / 30% Italian govt bonds (BTPs, MDUR 6.45); target: 60% stocks split €150M Italian/€150M German (β 0.9 vs DAX) / 40% Italian bonds €200M; needs to remove €100M Italian stock exposure, add €50M German stock exposure, add €50M Italian bond exposure; can use futures on FTSE MIB, DAX, and BTP.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Mario Rossi manages a €500 million portfolio that is allocated 70% to stocks and 30% to bonds. Over the next three-month horizon, he is bearish on eurozone stocks, except for German shares, and is bullish on Italian bonds. So, Rossi wants to reduce the overall allocation to stocks by 10%, to 60%, and achieve the same weight (30%) in Italian stocks (which have a beta of 1.1 with respect to the FTSE MIB Index) and German stocks (which have a beta of 0.9 with respect to the DAX index). He also wants to increase the overall allocation to Italian government bonds (BTPs) by 10%, to 40%. The bond portion of his portfolio has a modified duration of 6.45. In summary... Rossi needs to remove €100 million of exposure to Italian stocks, add €50 million of exposure to German stocks, and add €50 million of exposure to Italian bonds in his portfolio.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The derivatives overlay accomplishes the shift — futures on equities and bonds — without disturbing the cash positions.

questions.stem: *Grant rebalancing example* — target 40/60 stock/bond, portfolio grown to €106M with €46M stocks/€60M bonds, EURO STOXX 50 futures €35,000 per contract, β=1.2, target β=0 for reduction *most likely*:

options.A: no calc.
options.B: no data.
options.C: want to reduce stock exposure by €3.6M (from €46M to €42.4M).

rationales.A: Sell 124 stock futures.
rationales.B: Sell 124.
rationales.C:
Full statement: want to reduce stock exposure by €3.6M (from €46M to €42.4M); N_f = [(0.0 - 1.2)/1.0] × (€3,604,000/€35,000) = -123.57 → sell 124 futures contracts (rounded).

Correct. Per the reading: 'The market value of the European stocks is €46,000,000, and Grant wants to reduce the exposure to this market to 40% or 0.40(€106,000,000) = €42,400,000. She decides to sell enough futures contracts on the EURO STOXX 50 to reduce the allocation to European stocks by 0.034(€106,000,000) = €3,604,000. To achieve the desired reduction in exposure to European stocks, the market value of the stocks involved in the transaction (S) will be €3,604,000. The European stocks' beta (βS) is 1.2, and the target beta is βT = 0. The EURO STOXX 50 index futures have a contract value (F) of €35,000, with a beta (βf) of 1.0, so Nf = [(0.0 − 1.2)/1.0] × (€3,604,000/€35,000) = −123.57. Grant sells 124 futures contracts (after rounding).'

questions.stem: *Grant bond leg* — add €3.6M German bunds, MDUR target 9.5, CTD BPV €84.63, CF 0.619489 *most likely*:

options.B: no calc.
options.C: no data.

rationales.A:
Full statement: BPV_T = 9.5 × 0.0001 × €3,604,000 = €3,424; BPV_HR = [(BPV_T - 0)/BPV_CTD] × CF = [(€3,424 - 0)/€84.63] × 0.619489 = 25.06 ≈ 25 contracts; Grant should BUY 25 Euro-Bund futures contracts to gain €3.6M bond exposure at target duration 9.5.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The market value of the German bunds is €60,000,000, and Grant wants to increase the exposure to this market to 0.60(€106,000,000) = €63,600,000. She decides to purchase enough Euro-Bund futures so that €3,604,000 (= 0.034 × €106,000,000) of exposure to German bonds is added to the portfolio. The target basis point value (BPVT) is given by Equation 4, as follows: BPVT = MDURT × 0.01% × MVP = 9.5 × 0.0001 × €3,604,000 = €3,424. The cheapest-to-deliver bond has a BPVCTD of €84.63 and a conversion factor of 0.619489. The number of Euro-Bund futures to buy... is found using Equation 9: BPVHR = [(BPVT − BPVP)/BPVCTD] × CF = [(€3,424 − 0)/€84.63] × 0.619489 = 25.06 ≈ 25 contracts. So, Grant should buy 25 Euro-Bund futures contracts.'
rationales.B: Buy 25 bond futures.
rationales.C: Buy 25.

questions.stem: *Using derivatives to achieve exposures without disturbing underlying* *most likely* advantage:

options.A: no advantage.
options.B: derivatives can achieve targeted equity and interest rate risk exposures without disturbing underlying holdings.
options.C: no benefit.

rationales.A: Multiple benefits.
rationales.B:
Full statement: derivatives can achieve targeted equity and interest rate risk exposures without disturbing underlying holdings; benefits include: preserving tax bases (avoiding realized gains), maintaining voting rights (no ownership transfer), avoiding transaction costs of physical rebalancing, achieving desired exposures quickly and efficiently, and reversing positions relatively easily; Rossi example: he wants to change allocations quickly, and TMM example: 'TMM decides to execute a series of swaps that would enable it to change its position temporarily but more easily and less expensively than by executing the physical transactions.'

Correct. Per the reading (TMM section): 'TMM knows these changes would entail a considerable amount of trading in stocks and bonds. So, TMM decides to execute a series of swaps that would enable it to change its position temporarily but more easily and less expensively than by executing the physical transactions.' Preserving positions and avoiding tax consequences is also implicit in the Tundra example.
rationales.C: Efficiency + preservation.

questions.stem: *Total return swap replication of asset allocation change* *most likely*:

options.A: Asset allocation changes cannot be replicated with total return swaps.
options.B: The TMM example uses a single swap on one index to accomplish the entire reallocation.
options.C: e.g., TMM decides to increase allocation in large-cap sector by investing in S&P 500 Index, increase small-cap by investing in S&P SmallCap 600 Index (SPSC).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Replication is exactly what the example demonstrates.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The structure uses MULTIPLE legs — receiving S&P 500 (large-cap) and small-cap index returns while paying away exposures being reduced.
rationales.C:
Full statement: e.g., TMM decides to increase allocation in large-cap sector by investing in S&P 500 Index, increase small-cap by investing in S&P SmallCap 600 Index (SPSC); to reduce allocation in fixed-income sector, replicate performance of Bloomberg Barclays US Treasury Index (BBT) for government bond sector and BofA Merrill Lynch US Corporate Index (BAMLC) for corporate; equity swap: receives total return of S&P 500, pays floating rate tied to MRR minus agreed-on spread; fixed-income swap: pays total return of BBT, receives floating payments tied to MRR minus agreed-on spread.

Correct. Per the reading: 'TMM decides to increase the allocation in the large-cap sector by investing in the S&P 500 Index and to increase that in the small-cap sector by investing in the S&P SmallCap 600 Index (SPSC). To reduce the allocation in the overall fixed-income sector, TMM decides to replicate the performance of the Bloomberg Barclays US Treasury Index (BBT) for the government bond sector and the BofA Merrill Lynch US Corporate Index (BAMLC) for the corporate bond sector... Equity swap 1: TMM receives the total return of the S&P 500 and makes a floating payment tied to the market reference rate (MRR) minus the agreed-on spread, both on a notional principal of $27 million... Fixed-income swap 1: TMM pays the total return of the BBT and receives floating payments tied to the MRR minus the agreed-on spread, both on notional principal of $25 million.'

questions.stem: *Swap replication limitations* *most likely*:

options.A: transaction will not perfectly replicate performance of TMM's equity and fixed-income portfolios unless they are indexed to indexes selected as underlying of swaps.
options.B: Swap replication of an allocation change is always perfect regardless of how the portfolios are constructed.
options.C: The only limitation of swap-based replication is exchange margin requirements.

rationales.A:
Full statement: transaction will not perfectly replicate performance of TMM's equity and fixed-income portfolios unless they are indexed to indexes selected as underlying of swaps; TMM could encounter cash flow problem if its fixed-income payments exceed its equity receipts and its portfolio does not generate enough cash to fund its net obligation; stock and bond portfolio will generate cash only from dividends and interest; capital gains will not be received in cash unless portion of portfolio is sold — but avoiding selling portion of portfolio is very reason why TMM wants to use swaps.

Correct. Per the reading: 'It is important to recognize that this transaction will not perfectly replicate the performance of TMM's equity and fixed-income portfolios, unless they are indexed to the indexes selected as underlying of the swaps. In addition, TMM could encounter a cash flow problem if its fixed-income payments exceed its equity receipts and its portfolio does not generate enough cash to fund its net obligation. The stock and bond portfolio will generate cash only from dividends and interest. Capital gains will not be received in cash unless a portion of the portfolio is sold. But avoiding selling a portion of the portfolio is the very reason why TMM wants to use swaps.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Perfection requires the underlying portfolios to be INDEXED to the swap indexes — active portfolios leave tracking gaps.
rationales.C: Incorrect. OTC swaps carry no exchange margin; the limitations are tracking mismatch and cash flow timing.

questions.stem: *Cash equitization purpose* for indexed fund *most likely*:

options.A: no purpose.
options.B: cash equitization used to minimize tracking error risk.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Tracking error min.
rationales.B:
Full statement: cash equitization used to minimize tracking error risk; portfolio manager without expectation on direction of underlying market implements cash equitization strategy attempting to replicate performance of relevant index on cash reserves; McMillian's motive was to accomplish goal of minimizing tracking error by following this strategy; comparing outcomes with and without strategy shows 5% return vs 4.6% return demonstrates strategy effectiveness.

Correct. Per the reading: 'McMillian does not have an expectation on the direction of UK stocks over the next quarter. However, she is keen to minimize tracking error risk, so she implements a cash equitization strategy attempting to replicate the performance of the FTSE 100 on the cash reserves.' And: 'she accomplished her goal of minimizing tracking error by following this strategy.'
rationales.C: Tracking error.

questions.stem: *Portfolio manager unwinding swap position* *most likely*:

options.A: A swap position cannot be exited before its scheduled maturity under any circumstances.
options.B: Unwinding a swap requires physically delivering the underlying index constituents.
options.C: TMM could decide to unwind position before one year elapses, which it could do by executing new swap with opposite payments for remainder of life of original swap.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Early exit is routine — via an offsetting swap or negotiated termination.
rationales.B: Incorrect. No physical delivery is involved; TMM executes a NEW swap with opposite payments for the remaining term.
rationales.C:
Full statement: TMM could decide to unwind position before one year elapses, which it could do by executing new swap with opposite payments for remainder of life of original swap; also if TMM wishes to extend period, would need to renegotiate swap at expiration; provides flexibility to reverse or extend positions as market conditions change.

Correct. Per the reading: 'If it wishes to extend this period, TMM would need to renegotiate the swap at expiration. Likewise, TMM could decide to unwind the position before one year elapses, which it could do by executing a new swap with opposite payments for the remainder of the life of the original swap.'

drills.los_text: demonstrate the use of derivatives in asset allocation, rebalancing, and inferring market expectations

questions.stem: *Typical uses of swaps, forwards, and futures* *most likely* include:

options.A: modifying portfolio returns and risk exposures (hedging and directional bets) using interest rate/currency/equity swaps and futures, fixed-income futures, variance swaps.
options.B: Swaps, forwards, and futures serve no purpose in modern portfolio management.
options.C: The only use of these derivatives is short-term speculation on price direction.

rationales.A:
Full statement: modifying portfolio returns and risk exposures (hedging and directional bets) using interest rate/currency/equity swaps and futures, fixed-income futures, variance swaps; creating desired payoffs using forwards, futures, total return swaps; performing asset allocation and portfolio rebalancing using equity index futures, government bond futures, index swaps; inferring market expectations for interest rates, inflation, and volatility using Fed funds futures, inflation swaps, VIX futures.

Correct. Per the reading table: 'Common Uses of Swaps, Forwards, and Futures | Typical Derivatives Used | Modifying Portfolio Returns and Risk Exposures (Hedging and Directional Bets) | Interest Rate, Currency, and Equity Swaps and Futures; Fixed-Income Futures; Variance Swaps | Creating Desired Payoffs | Forwards, Futures, Total Return Swaps | Performing Asset Allocation and Portfolio Rebalancing | Equity Index Futures, Government Bond Futures, Index Swaps | Inferring Market Expectations for Interest Rates, Inflation, and Volatility | Fed Funds Futures, Inflation Swaps, VIX Futures.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. They are core implementation tools across hedging, exposure modification, and allocation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Speculation is one corner; risk modification, hedging, rebalancing, and inferring market expectations complete the map.

questions.stem: *Rebalancing via futures* Grant example rationale *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: Grant manages portfolio with target 40% stocks/60% bonds.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Rebalance to target.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Grant manages portfolio with target 40% stocks/60% bonds; portfolio grew from €100M to €106M so current €46M stocks (43.4%) and €60M bonds (56.6%); wants to rebalance back to target 40/60 (€42.4M stocks, €63.6M bonds); will use stock index futures and bond futures to achieve objective; once notional values to be traded are known, determines how many futures contracts should be purchased or sold to achieve desired asset allocation.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Yolanda Grant manages a portfolio with a target allocation of 40% in stocks and 60% in bonds. Over the last month, the value of the portfolio has increased from €100 million to €106 million, and Grant wants to rebalance it back to the target allocation (40% stocks/60% bonds). As shown in Exhibit 6, the current portfolio has €46 million (43.4%) in European stocks (with beta of 1.2 with respect to the EURO STOXX 50 index) and €60 million (56.6%) in German bunds. The bonds have a modified duration of 9.5... Grant will use stock index futures and bond futures to achieve this objective. Once the notional values to be traded are known, she determines how many futures contracts should be purchased or sold to achieve the desired asset allocation.'
rationales.C: Rebalance.

questions.stem: *Fed funds futures for inferring FOMC expectations* *most likely*:

options.A: Market expectations for FOMC policy are inferred primarily from equity index dividend futures.
options.B: No traded instrument allows inference of expected Federal Reserve policy moves.
options.C: commonly followed metric is probability of change in federal funds rate at upcoming FOMC meetings implied by prices of fed funds futures contracts.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Dividend futures price payout streams, not policy rates.
rationales.B: Incorrect. FED FUNDS FUTURES are the canonical instrument — their prices imply probabilities of rate changes at upcoming meetings.
rationales.C:
Full statement: commonly followed metric is probability of change in federal funds rate at upcoming FOMC meetings implied by prices of fed funds futures contracts; when US central bank began rate hiking cycle in 2015, declared intention to maintain 25 bp target range (lower and upper bounds) for federal funds rate; Fed regularly communicates 'forward guidance' along with 'dot plot' which shows where each FOMC meeting participant believes federal funds rate should be at end of year for next few years and in longer run; implied probabilities represent market's view and may diverge from Fed's guidance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A commonly followed metric is the probability of a change in the federal funds rate at upcoming FOMC meetings that is implied by the prices of fed funds futures contracts. When the US central bank began its rate hiking cycle in 2015, it declared an intention to maintain a 25 bp "target range" (lower and upper bounds) for the federal funds rate. The Fed regularly communicates its "forward guidance" along with the so-called dot plot, which shows where each FOMC meeting participant believes the federal funds rate should be at the end of the year, for the next few years, and in the longer run.'

questions.stem: *Fed funds futures pricing formula* *most likely*:

options.B: no formula.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
fed funds futures traded on Chicago Board of Trade; contract price quoted as 100 minus market's expectation for effective federal funds (FFE) rate: Fed funds futures contract price = 100 - Expected FFE rate; at expiration, contract is cash settled to simple average (overnight) effective federal funds rate for delivery month; overnight rate calculated and reported daily by Federal Reserve Bank of New York; underlying assumption is that implied futures market rates are predicting monthly average effective federal funds rate.

Correct. Per Equation 17: 'Fed funds futures are traded on the Chicago Board of Trade, and the contract price is quoted as 100 minus the market's expectation for the FFE rate, as follows: Fed funds futures contract price = 100 − Expected FFE rate. At expiration, the contract is cash settled to the simple average (overnight) effective federal funds rate for the delivery month. The overnight rate is calculated and reported daily by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.'
rationales.B: 100 - Expected FFE.
rationales.C: Price = 100 - rate.

questions.stem: *Probability of rate hike formula* *most likely*:

options.A: P(hike) = Current federal funds rate / FFE rate implied by futures.
options.B: P(hike) = (FFE rate implied by futures - Current federal funds rate) / (Federal funds rate assuming rate hike - Current federal funds rate).
options.C: P(hike) = FFE rate implied by futures × Federal funds rate assuming a hike.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A simple ratio of levels carries no probability content.
rationales.B:
Full statement: P(hike) = (FFE rate implied by futures - Current federal funds rate) / (Federal funds rate assuming rate hike - Current federal funds rate); current federal funds rate is midpoint of current target range.

Correct. Per Equation 18: 'To determine the probability of a change in the federal funds rate, the following formula is used, where the current federal funds rate is the midpoint of the current target range: (Effective federal funds rate implied by futures contract − Current federal funds rate) / (Federal funds rate assuming a rate hike − Current federal funds rate).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The formula interpolates: (FFE implied − current rate) / (post-hike rate − current rate).

questions.stem: *Okyung fed funds probability calc* fed funds futures price 97.90, current target range 1.75%-2.00% *most likely*:

options.A: no calc.
options.B: no data.

rationales.A: 90% probability.
rationales.B: 90% hike.
rationales.C:
Full statement: FFE implied = 100 - 97.90 = 2.10%; current midpoint = 1.875%; assumed new range 2.00%-2.25% with midpoint 2.125%; P(hike) = (2.10% - 1.875%) / (2.125% - 1.875%) = 0.225/0.25 = 0.90 or 90%; high probability of 25 bp rate hike.

Correct. Per Example: 'the current price for the fed funds futures contract expiring after the next FOMC meeting is 97.90. The current federal funds rate target range is set between 1.75% and 2.00%... The FFE rate implied by the futures contract price is 2.10% (= 100 − 97.90)... given that the FFE rate embedded in the fed funds futures price is 2.10%, there is a high probability that the FOMC will increase rates by 25 bps from its current target range of 1.75%–2.00% to the new target range of 2.00%–2.25%. Given the Fed's declared incremental move size of 25 bps, he calculates the probability of a rate hike as (2.100% − 1.875%)/(2.125% − 1.875%) = 0.90, or 90% where 1.875% is the midpoint of the current target range (1.75%–2.00%) and 2.125% is the midpoint of the new target range (2.00%–2.25%) assuming a rate hike.'

questions.stem: *End-of-month bias in FFE rate* *most likely*:

options.B: no bias.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
typical end-of-month (EOM) activity by large financial and banking institutions often induces 'dips' in FFE rate that create bias issues when using rate as basis for probability calculations of potential FOMC rate moves; if such activity increased price for relevant fed funds futures contract to 98.05, FFE rate would decline to 1.95% (= 100 - 98.05); using same equation, probability of FOMC rate hike decreases from 90% to just 30%: (1.950 - 1.875)/0.25 = 0.30; data providers implement smoothing methods.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Importantly, typical end-of-month (EOM) activity by large financial and banking institutions often induces "dips" in the FFE rate that create bias issues when using the rate as the basis for probability calculations of potential FOMC rate moves. For example, if such activity increased the price for the relevant fed funds futures contract to 98.05, then the FFE rate would decline to 1.95% (= 100 − 98.05). In this case, using the same equation as before, the probability of an FOMC rate hike decreases from 90% to just 30%: (1.950 − 1.875)/0.25 = 0.30, or 30%. To overcome this end-of-month bias, data providers have implemented various methods of "smoothing" the EOM dips.'
rationales.B: EOM dips.
rationales.C: EOM smoothing.

questions.stem: *Smoothing method* for EOM bias *most likely*:

options.A: No method exists to correct fed funds futures inference for end-of-month rate distortions.
options.B: one prominent data provider uses method that builds forward rate structure based on where market believes interest rates will settle in non-FOMC meeting months and then uses these forward rates to make appropriate adjustments.
options.C: The smoothing correction discards futures data entirely in favor of survey-based expectations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A prominent data provider's smoothing method addresses exactly this bias.
rationales.B:
Full statement: one prominent data provider uses method that builds forward rate structure based on where market believes interest rates will settle in non-FOMC meeting months and then uses these forward rates to make appropriate adjustments; for each FOMC meeting month, assumed that effective rate prevails until meeting date and then some rate prevails after meeting, with average effective rate over month being implied by futures price.

Correct. Per the reading: 'One prominent data provider uses a method that builds a forward rate structure based on where the market believes interest rates will settle in non-FOMC meeting months and then uses these forward rates to make appropriate adjustments. For each FOMC meeting month, it is assumed that an effective rate prevails until the meeting date, and then some rate prevails after the meeting, with the average effective rate over the month being implied by the futures price.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Futures remain the input — the method builds a forward rate structure from where the market expects rates to be, smoothing EOM artifacts.

questions.stem: *Divergence between market expectations and Fed forward guidance* *most likely*:

options.A: always aligned.
options.B: no distinction.
options.C: implied market expectations can diverge significantly from Fed's forward guidance.

rationales.A: Market vs Fed dots.
rationales.B: Divergence possible.
rationales.C:
Full statement: implied market expectations can diverge significantly from Fed's forward guidance; implied probabilities represent market's view and may diverge from guidance provided by central banks in their regular communications about likely future course of monetary policy actions; especially for longer-term horizon, these inferred probabilities usually do not have strong predictive power; the 'dot plot' shows where each FOMC meeting participant believes federal funds rate should be at end of year for next few years and in longer run.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Note that such implied probabilities represent the market's view and may diverge from the guidance provided by central banks in their regular communications about the likely future course of monetary policy actions. Furthermore, especially for the longer-term horizon, these inferred probabilities usually do not have strong predictive power.' And: 'implied market expectations (dotted line) can diverge significantly from the Fed's forward guidance (solid line, the median of the dots).'

questions.stem: *Inferences represent current expectations of future events* *most likely*:

options.A: important to emphasize that inferences from derivatives relate to current expectations of future events — they do NOT foretell what will actually happen — which can change with the arrival of new information.
options.B: Derivatives-implied probabilities reliably foretell the actual outcome of future central bank decisions.
options.C: Inferences from derivatives prices are meaningless noise with no informational content.

rationales.A:
Full statement: important to emphasize that inferences from derivatives relate to current expectations of future events — they do NOT foretell what will actually happen — which can change with the arrival of new information; useful for gauging market sentiment and pricing expectations but not for predicting actual outcomes with certainty.

Correct. Per the reading: 'It is important to emphasize that these inferences relate to current expectations of future events — they do not foretell what will actually happen — which can change with the arrival of new information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. They are CURRENT EXPECTATIONS, not predictions — outcomes routinely diverge from priced-in paths.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The information is real and closely watched; the caveat is its nature as a snapshot of expectations.

questions.stem: *Sum of probabilities* at each FOMC meeting *most likely*:

options.A: The probabilities of all possible rate outcomes at a given FOMC meeting sum to more than one when uncertainty is high.
options.B: probability of rate hike or cut is represented as probability of move from current target range at specified meeting date, and probabilities of all three actions (hike/no change/cut) at a particular meeting date sum to 1.
options.C: Probabilities across meeting outcomes are unrelated to one another.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The outcome probabilities always sum to EXACTLY one — they exhaust the possibilities.
rationales.B:
Full statement: probability of rate hike or cut is represented as probability of move from current target range at specified meeting date, and probabilities of all three actions (hike/no change/cut) at a particular meeting date sum to 1; data providers show trends in implied probabilities over time.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Note that the probability of a rate hike or cut is represented as the probability of a move from the current target range at the specified meeting date, and of course, the probabilities of all three actions at a particular meeting date sum to 1.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. They are jointly constrained: move probabilities plus the no-change probability complete the unit mass.

questions.stem: *Changing allocations via TMM swap example* structure *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: TMM invested $200M as 75% stocks, 25% bonds.

rationales.A: TMM structure.
rationales.B: TMM detailed.
rationales.C:
Full statement: TMM invested $200M as 75% stocks, 25% bonds; wants 90% stocks/10% bonds; equity target: $27M more S&P 500 (large-cap 60%→65%), $3M more S&P SmallCap 600 (10% unchanged); bond target: sell $25M BBT (govt), sell $5M BAMLC (corporate); TMM structures swaps to have all payments occur on same dates six months apart with maturity of one year; if desired to extend, must renegotiate at expiration; unwinding possible by opposite swap.

Correct. Per the reading: 'To achieve the desired asset allocation, TMM takes the following exposures: A long position of $27 million in the S&P 500. A long position of $3 million in the SPSC. A short position of $25 million in the BBT. A short position of $5 million in the BAMLC. The mid-cap exposure of $45 million does not change, so TMM does not need to incorporate a mid-cap index into the swap. TMM uses a combination of equity and fixed-income swaps to achieve its target allocation and structures the swap to have all payments occur on the same dates six months apart. TMM also decides that the swap should mature in one year. If it wishes to extend this period, TMM would need to renegotiate the swap at expiration. Likewise, TMM could decide to unwind the position before one year elapses, which it could do by executing a new swap with opposite payments for the remainder of the life of the original swap.'
